message.abstract:
<jats:title>Abstract</jats:title>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Introduction</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>Two waves of COVID-19 cases have overwhelmed most European countries during 2020. It is unclear if the incidence of acute kidney injury (AKI) has changed during the COVID-19 outbreaks. This study aims to evaluate the differences in incidence, risk factors and outcome of AKI in patients with SARS-CoV-2 infection during the first and second wave of COVID-19.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Method</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>We reviewed the health medical records of 792 consecutive patients with COVID-19 hospitalized at the University Hospital of Modena, Italy, from February 25 to December 14, 2020.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Results</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>AKI was diagnosed in 122 (15.4%) patients. Incidence of AKI remained steady rate during wave-1 (15.9%) and wave-2 (14.7%) (P=0.89). AKI patients were older (P=<0.001) and had a more severe respiratory impairment (PO2/FO2) (P=≤0.001) than their non-AKI counterparts. AKI led to a longer hospital stay (P=0.001), complicated with a higher rate of ICU admission. COVID-19-related AKI was associate with 59.7% of deaths during wave-1 and 70.6% during wave-2. At the end of the period of observation, 24% (wave-1) and 46.7% (wave-2) of survivors were discharged with a not fully recovered kidney function. Risk factors for AKI in patients with COVID-19 were diuretics (HR=5.3; 95%CI, 1.2-23.3; P=0.025) and cardiovascular disease (HR, 2.23; 95%CI, 1.05-5.1; P=0.036).</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Conclusion</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>The incidence of AKI (about 15%) remained unchanged during 2020, regardless of the trend of COVID-19. AKI occurred in patients with severe COVID-19 symptoms and was associated with a higher incidence of deaths than non-AKI patients. The risk factors of COVID-19-related AKI were diuretic therapy and cardiovascular disease.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
title: Similar Rates of AKI during the First Two Waves of COVID-19 in Northern Italy: a single-center study
